reason: Could not construct a concrete refutation; every claim verifies against the files. (1) UNDER-FLAGGING: the prompt's new scope exclusion ('a new component not yet wired into a parent is NOT a usability defect') targets exactly the spurious 'DeleteButton is exported but never used' over-flag that appeared on the GOOD fixture (findings/usability/good/delete-with-confirm__r0). The bad fixture's defects (no-confirm, no loading/disabled state, no error branch, unconditional window.location.reload on non-ok responses) are wiring-independent and were flagged 3/3 repeats (r0,r1,r2). The rewrite ALSO adds 'low' for concrete defects (silent mutation failure, no error branch), strengthening recall, not weakening it. No bad-recall regression. (2) GATE-SLIP: usability is advisory in the engine -- registry.py:291-296 sets block_threshold=None (4th positional arg) -- so it NEVER blocks; there is no gate for a known-bad to slip through, and the bad fixture is correctly flagged every repeat. (3) DIRTY-GOOD-TUNING: usability-001 makes NO good-fixture edit and sets needs_second_good_fixture=false; the lone good fixture (delete-with-confirm) is genuinely clean and well-formed (has diff --git/index header, full confirm+loading+error handling). Its only baseline findings are info-tier taste, which the rubric explicitly defines as non-FP for advisory agents. The fp 0.33 is verifiably a thresholds.yaml mismatch (usability held to fp_max:0.10 blocking semantics while registry block_threshold=None), correctly deferred to usability-calibration-001 -- not a precision problem. (4) FIXTURE-EDIT AMBIGUITY: the sole fixture edit ('regenerate-diff-header' on bad/delete-no-confirm) is justified -- the bad change.patch genuinely lacks the 'diff --git'/'index' header that the good change.patch has, and baseline line anchors wobbled (37/37/33 r0, 30/28/28/29 r1, 37/37/32/33 r2); the edit makes anchoring consistent and explicitly 'do NOT change the defect', so it does not make the bad case ambiguous nor the good case dirty. Default-to-refuted applies only under uncertainty; here the evidence is unambiguous, so refuted=false is warranted.
